mcqs.question: What is the fundamental architectural difference between shared-memory and distributed-memory systems?

options.text: The number of processors used.
The programming language used for development.
The presence or absence of a global address space.
The clock speed of the individual processors.

mcqs.explanation: The core distinction lies in the memory model. Shared-memory systems have a single, global address space accessible by all processors, while distributed-memory systems have multiple, private address spaces.

mcqs.question: In a UMA (Uniform Memory Access) shared-memory system, what is true about memory access times?

options.text: Local memory is faster than remote memory.
All processors have equal access time to all memory locations.
Only one processor can access memory at a time.
Memory access requires explicit message passing.

mcqs.explanation: UMA (also called SMP -- Symmetric Multiprocessing) provides equal memory access time to all processors. This contrasts with NUMA where access time depends on the proximity of the memory to the requesting processor.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a primary advantage of distributed-memory systems over shared-memory systems?

options.text: Easier to program due to implicit communication.
Lower hardware cost and better scalability for large systems.
Faster access to all memory locations for all processors.
No need for explicit synchronization mechanisms.

mcqs.explanation: Distributed-memory systems (e.g., clusters) are built from commodity hardware, making them cost-effective and highly scalable to thousands of cores without the bus bandwidth bottleneck of shared-memory systems.

mcqs.question: In a shared-memory system, what is the primary purpose of a cache coherence protocol?

options.text: To compress data in memory to save space.
To manage the distribution of tasks among processors.
To ensure all processor caches have a consistent view of shared data.
To encrypt data stored in memory for security.

mcqs.explanation: Cache coherence protocols (like MESI) ensure that when one processor writes to a memory location, other processors holding stale cached copies are notified, maintaining data consistency across all caches.

mcqs.question: A modern supercomputer comprises nodes, each with two 64-core CPUs and local RAM. Communication between nodes uses InfiniBand. Which programming model is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Pure OpenMP for all cores.
Pure MPI, launching one process per core.
A hybrid model using MPI between nodes and OpenMP within a node.
Pure Pthreads for all cores.

mcqs.explanation: This describes a hybrid architecture. MPI handles inter-node communication (distributed memory), while OpenMP handles intra-node parallelism (shared memory within each 128-core node), combining the strengths of both models.

mcqs.question: What is a major challenge when programming for distributed-memory systems that is less of an issue in shared-memory systems?

options.text: Preventing race conditions on shared variables.
Manually partitioning data and managing communication between processes.
The overhead of hardware cache coherence.
Ensuring all processors have equal access time to memory (UMA).

mcqs.explanation: In distributed-memory systems, the programmer must explicitly decompose the problem, partition data across nodes, and handle all send/receive communication. This is much more complex than shared-memory where data sharing is implicit.

mcqs.question: Which statement correctly contrasts synchronization needs in shared vs. distributed memory systems?

options.text: Only shared-memory systems require any form of synchronization.
Shared-memory uses locks and barriers for synchronization; distributed-memory synchronizes implicitly through send/receive message ordering.
Distributed-memory systems need more synchronization than shared-memory systems.
Neither architecture requires synchronization if the algorithm is designed correctly.

mcqs.explanation: In shared-memory systems, threads must use explicit synchronization primitives (locks, barriers, semaphores) to coordinate access to shared data. In distributed-memory systems, much of the synchronization is handled implicitly by the message passing operations themselves (e.g., a blocking receive waits for the matching send).
